coredata.dc:title: Photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline by N-CQDs modified S-g-C3N4 nanotubes and its product toxicity evaluation

coredata.dc:description: In this study, to improve the electron-hole pairs separation rate and enhance the antibiotic wastewater treatment efficiency, the sulfur doped g-C3N4 (SCN) nanotubes were modified with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) derived from pig manure by a simple solvothermal method, and SCN/N-CQDs composite photocatalyst was synthesized. SCN with hexagonal tubular structure has strong light scattering ability and abundant active sites. At the same time, the modification of N-CQDs extended the visible light response range, accelerated the separation of electron-hole pairs, and realized the efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC). The SCN/N-CQDs-1 showed the best photocatalytic performance, and degradation rate of TC could reach to 79.11% after 90 min of visible light irradiation, much higher than the degradation efficiency of pure SCN (58.33%). Among them, the generation of hole, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals play an important role in the efficient degradation of TC. Furthermore, the intermediates produced during the degradation process were detected by ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC − MS), and the possible TC degradation pathways were investigated. The repeatability and practicability of SCN/N-CQDs are proved by five cycle experiments. In addition, compared with the original toxicity, the inhibition rate of Escherichia coli (E. coli) colony growth was reduced by 91.315% after 90 min photodegradation. The results of biotoxicity analysis showed that SCN/N-CQDs leaching solution and its photodegradation wastewater did not produce biotoxicity to E. coli.

(a) XPS survey spectra of S-g-C3N4 and S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs-1 composites and the corresponding high-resolution XPS spectra (b) C 1 s, (c) N 1 s, (d) S 2p of S-g-C3N4 and S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs-1 composites.
SEM images of S-g-C3N4 (a, b, c); S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs (d, e, f).
EDS spectra of S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs (a) and corresponding element mapping of N (b), C (c), and O (d).

UV–vis diffused reflectance spectra of g-C3N4, S-g-C3N4 and S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs-1 (a); Photoluminescence of N-CQDs (b); Band gap energy (c) and XPS valence (d) of g-C3N4 and S-g-C3N4; Photoluminescence spectra (e); Time-resolved fluorescence spectra (f); I-t curves (g) and EIS response (h) of g-C3N4, S-g-C3N4 and S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs-1.

(a) The photocatalytic degradation performance of TC and (b) the first–order–kinetics rate constant of TC over photocatalysts under visible light irradiation (Conditions: catalyst = 20 mg, TC = 20 mg/L, visible light λ ≥ 400 nm, Natural pH).

Effect of (a) pollutant concentration (Conditions: catalyst = 20 mg, visible light λ ≥ 400 nm, Natural pH); (b) catalyst dosage (Conditions: TC = 20 mg/L, visible light λ ≥ 400 nm, Natural pH); (c) solution pH and (d) coexisting anions (Conditions: TC = 20 mg/L, catalyst = 20 mg, visible light λ ≥ 400 nm) on the removal efficiency of TC by S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs.

Trapping experiment for the photocatalytic degradation of TC over S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs (a); ESR spectra of S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs for DMPO − O2 − (b), DMPO − OH (c), TEMPO–h+ (d) in the dark and under visible light irradiation.
Schematic of photocatalytic process for TC degradation over S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs under visible light irradiation.
Proposed degradation pathways and intermediates of TC by S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs.

The recycled testing of S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs for organic pollutants degradation under visible light irradiation (a); and the XRD pattern (b); XPS survey spectrum (c), high–resolution spectra of C 1 s (d), N 1 s (e) and S 2p (f) of the S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs after 5th cycle for TC degradation.

Growth curves of E.coli treated with different concentrations of S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs (0 mg/mL, 0.5 mg/mL,1 mg/mL, 2 mg/mL) for 24 h (a), Photographs of E.coli bacterial colonies formed on LB-agar plates (degradation times: blank group, bulk TC, 0 min, 30 min, 60 min and 90 min) (b), The inhibition rate of E.coli colonies after culturing in solid medium for 14 h (c).
Summary of the analyses of the time-resolved transient PL decay spectra of the as-prepared samples.
Electrochemical parameters of equivalent circuit elements for the samples.

head.ce:title: Photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline by N-CQDs modified S-g-C3N4 nanotubes and its product toxicity evaluation

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, to improve the electron-hole pairs separation rate and enhance the antibiotic wastewater treatment efficiency, the sulfur doped g-C3N4 (SCN) nanotubes were modified with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) derived from pig manure by a simple solvothermal method, and SCN/N-CQDs composite photocatalyst was synthesized. SCN with hexagonal tubular structure has strong light scattering ability and abundant active sites. At the same time, the modification of N-CQDs extended the visible light response range, accelerated the separation of electron-hole pairs, and realized the efficient photocatalytic degradation of tetracycline (TC). The SCN/N-CQDs-1 showed the best photocatalytic performance, and degradation rate of TC could reach to 79.11% after 90 min of visible light irradiation, much higher than the degradation efficiency of pure SCN (58.33%). Among them, the generation of hole, hydroxyl radicals and superoxide radicals play an important role in the efficient degradation of TC. Furthermore, the intermediates produced during the degradation process were detected by ultra-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC − MS), and the possible TC degradation pathways were investigated. The repeatability and practicability of SCN/N-CQDs are proved by five cycle experiments. In addition, compared with the original toxicity, the inhibition rate of Escherichia coli (E. coli) colony growth was reduced by 91.315% after 90 min photodegradation. The results of biotoxicity analysis showed that SCN/N-CQDs leaching solution and its photodegradation wastewater did not produce biotoxicity to E. coli.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Tetracycline (TC) antibiotics have broad-spectrum bacteriostatic effects and are widely used in human therapy, veterinary use, and the agricultural sector [1,2]. As they are difficult to be fully absorbed and digested by humans and organisms, a large fraction of the original TC and its metabolites are excreted and accumulated in the environment [3]. TC remaining in the environment can induce the production of resistant bacteria and resistant genes, thus causing serious effects on ecosystem function [4,5]. Therefore, more and more researchers' have begun to focus on the management of TC antibiotic pollution in ecosystems. However, traditional TC removal methods such as physical method, chemical method and biological method are difficult to achieve the ideal degradation effect, the process is complicated, the cost is high, and the degradation process is easy to cause secondary pollution [6,7].

In recent years, photocatalytic technology has become an ideal method for removing tetracycline antibiotics due to its high efficiency, low energy consumption and no secondary pollution [8,9]. More striking is that the redox process of two-dimensional semiconductors (such as graphene [10], hexagonal boron nitriding, two-dimensional black phosphorus, two-dimensional MOF and Carbon nitride (g-C3N4) [11], etc.) has become a new strategy for utilizing solar energy in the field of wastewater treatment [12]. Among them, g-C3N4, as a polymer semiconductor with graphene-like structure, is considered as one of the promising semiconductor materials in photocatalysis technology due to its suitable band gap, low toxicity and high stability [13,14]. But g-C3N4 also has very obvious shortcomings: high recombination rate of charge carrier, low conductivity, insufficient light absorption, relatively small specific surface area [15-17]. Therefore, researchers improved the photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 by precious metal deposition, semiconductor recombination, metal/non-metal doping and other methods [18]. Studies have shown that the synergistic effect of sulfur atoms and g-C3N4 can adjust the electron and band structure of g-C3N4, increase the surface active sites, and improve the charge separation efficiency, thus achieving the purpose of enhancing the photocatalytic performance [19]. Xu et al. [20] proposed that the doped impurity level was located below the conduction band (CB), and the doped S atom could provide electrons to the CB of g-C3N4, widen the light absorption range, and improve the conductivity and catalytic activity of the material. Li et al. [21] used porous silica spheres as templates to synthesize meso/macroporous g-C3N4 spheres (SMCN) doped with sulfur with continuous channels. It is proved that the synergistic effect of sulfur doping and pore formation significantly increases the surface area of the material and improves the charge separation efficiency. Under visible light irradiation, H2 production rate and phenol degradation rate of SMCN are 15 times and 5 times higher than that of pure g-C3N4, respectively. However, the fast recombination rate of photogenerated electron holes and low light utilization efficiency of g-C3N4 and its derivatives still hinder its application in the field of photocatalysis [22].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a non-toxic and harmless green photocatalytic material, have unique optical and electronic properties [23,24]. In particular, its excellent electron transfer ability and up-conversion photoluminescence performance can not only improve the utilization efficiency of sunlight, but also promote the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers [12,25,26]. Therefore, they are widely introduced into traditional semiconductor materials to improve the catalytic activity of g-C3N4 [27], TiO2 [28], ZnO [29], CuS [30] and other semiconductors. Among them, many CQDS-modified g-C3N4, such as CNNS/CQDs [31], CQDs/g-C3N4 [32], CQDs/CNCs [33], have been widely used in the application of photocatalysis. For example, Li et al. [31] prepared CNNS/CQDs composites by modifying carbon nitride nanosheets (CNNS) with CQDs. Under visible light irradiation, the composite showed better hydrogen production performance than pure CNNS. The optimal H2 production rate for CNNS/CQDs was 116.1 μmol∙h−1, three times that of CNNS. In addition, Liu et al. [33] found that g-C3N4 (CNCs) decorated with CQDs also had excellent photocatalytic activity on the degradation of organic pollutants in water. Within 60 min, CNCs of DCF model has the highest removal efficiency is 100%, the rate constant (k1) is pure g-C3N4 (4.9 × 10−3 min−1) to 15 times. Compared with CQDs, N-CQDs rich in defect sites on the surface have more advantages in inducing photogenerated carrier separation and expanding the optical absorption range, which can more effectively promote the REDOX reaction of semiconductors [34,35]. For example, Chen et al. [36] proposed that the coupling of g-C3N4 catalyst and N-CQDs could drive the activation of PDS under visible light, so as to achieve the purpose of efficient oxidative degradation of HTC in water. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the degradation efficiency of N-CQDs modified g-C3N4 for tetracycline wastewater was as high as 91%. However, to our knowledge, no systematic studies on N-CQDs modification of S-g-C3N4 have been reported.

Therefore, in this study, biomass derived N-CQDs was modified on the surface of S-g-C3N4 nanotubes by solvothermal method, and S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs binary semiconductor composite photocatalyst with high catalytic activity was formed. And the photocatalytic performance of the S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs composite was measured by the study of degrading tetracycline in water. Using a series of characterization techniques such as XRD, BET, SEM, TEM, XPS, FTIR, UV–Vis DRS, etc., the morphology, structure and optical properties of S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs composite photocatalysts were investigated in detail. Then, the optimal degradation conditions were obtained by discussing the effects of N-CQDs addition amount, catalyst dosage, initial TC concentration, initial pH value, coexistence of anions and the effects of humus on the photodegradation efficiency of tetracycline. The stability and reproducibility of the S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs catalyst were investigated by 5 repeated cycle experiments. And through free radical capture experiment and electron spin resonance analysis, reasonable photocatalytic degradation mechanism is proposed. The intermediates produced in the degradation process were detected by ultra-high performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), and the possible degradation path of TC was proposed. Finally, using Escherichia coli（E. coli） as a biological model, the biotoxicity of S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs photodegradation TC products was studied through its growth curve and plate count, providing a theory for ecological risk assessment and practical application of S-g-C3N4/N-CQDs composites in accordance with.

Tetracycline (TC), Melamine, Thiourea were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. All other analytical grade chemicals and the ultrapure water resistivity at 18.25MΩ cm were used throughout the experiments.

Melamine and thiourea were used as the precursors of S-g-C3N4. Typically, 2 g melamine and 0.5 g thiourea were added to 40 mL ultrapure water for ultrasonic dissolution, and then transferred to a stainless steel reactor with Teflon for hydrothermal reaction at 180℃ for 24 h. The reaction was carried out at 180℃ for 24 h, and the obtained product was centrifuged and washed with water for three times, and then placed in a 105℃ oven to dry completely. The dried solid was heated to 550℃ at a rate of 4℃/min in a muffle furnace for reaction 4 h. After that, it is allowed to cool naturally, and the obtained block is put into a mortar and ground into yellow powder. The obtained S-g-C3N4 is labeled as SCN.

The N-CQDs was synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal method, in which the pig manure was used as the carbon and nitrogen source. Firstly, the pig manure was dried at 105℃ and ground to pass 100-mesh sieve. Then, 2 g of pig manure was added to 40 mL of ultrapure water. After stirring evenly, it was transferred into a 50 mL stainless steel reactor lined with Teflon for 6 h hydrothermal reaction at 200℃. After the reaction kettle was cooled to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm membrane. The obtained liquid was dialyzed in a 500 Da dialysis bag for 48 h. Finally, the obtained brown nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots was stored in a 4℃ refrigerator for later use.

90 mg of SCN was added to 40 mL absolute ethanol and sonicated for 1 h. Then, 0.1, 1, 3, 5 and 7 mL N-CQDs solution was added to the above solution respectively. After sonication for 1 h, the mixture was added to a 50 mL stainless steel reactor lined with Teflon and reacted at 120℃ for 2 h. The synthesized product was cooled to room temperature, washed three times, and dried at 105℃ for 12 h. The obtained samples are labeled as SCN/N-CQDs-0.1, SCN/N-CQDs-1, SCN/N-CQDs-3 and SCN/N-CQDs-5, SCN/N- CQDs-7, respectively.

The morphology and microstructure of the materials were observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM, ZEISS Sigma 300, Germany) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-2100F, Japan), and the element distribution was analyzed by SEM corresponding energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). The crystalline phase of the material was recorded by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, 6100, Japan). The elemental composition of the material was measured by X-ray electron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20, USA) was used to study the structural composition of the material or to determine its chemical groups. The specific surface area and average pore diameter of the material were measured by N2 adsorption–desorption specific surface analyzer (BET, Mack 3020, USA). The light absorption properties of the catalysts were analyzed in the range of 200–800 nm using ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, Shimadzu UV-3600i Plus, Japan). The photoluminescence spectrum of the material was measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (PL, Edinburgh FLS1000, England), and the electron-hole pair recombination of the material was measured. The photocurrent transient response (I-t) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests were performed at the electrochemical workstation (RST5200) to further investigate the photoinduced charge separation and transfer efficiency of the composite.

In order to study the photocatalytic performance under visible light irradiation of the synthesized materials, TC was used as the target pollutant in this experiment. The light source used in the experiment is a xenon lamp with a power of 300 W (CEL-HXF300, Beijing CEL Technology Co., Ltd.). The light intensity is 300 mW/cm2, and a cut-off filter (λ > 400 nm) is installed to simulate a visible light source. A 250 mL quartz glass beaker was used as the container. During the degradation process, 20 mg of catalyst was added to 100 mL of TC solution (10 mg/L) under magnetic stirring for 60 min to reach the adsorption–desorption equilibrium. In addition, the initial pollutant concentration (10, 15, 20 and 30 mg/L), catalyst dosage (0,10, 20, 30, 40 mg), pH (3.0–9.0), anion (Cl−、SO4 2−、NO3 − and PO4 3−) and humic acid (HA) (0, 10, 20, 30 mg) as a single factor experiment to study the effect of different factors on the photocatalytic performance. The reproducibility and stability of the catalyst were determined according to the change of TC degradation efficiency after repeated experiment for 5 times. For the first time, 120 mg catalyst was added into 600 mL tetracycline aqueous solution (10 mg/L) for photocatalytic degradation experiment. After each photocatalytic degradation, the remaining solution was centrifuged to collect the catalyst powder and washed with ultra-pure water for 3 times to remove the residual TC impurities. After drying at 60℃ in a vacuum drying oven, the catalyst powder was recovered, and then the photocatalytic degradation experiment was carried out under the same conditions. During the photoreaction, 3 mL of the reaction solution was withdrawn every 10 min and filtered through a 0.45 μm filter. The residual tetracycline concentration was measured at 357 nm with an ultraviolet–visible spectrophotometer (Specord 50, Germany). All photocatalytic experiments were repeated three times and the reproducibility of the results was within 5% of the relative standard deviation. A high performance liquid chromatography mass spectrometer (HPLC-MS, Thermo Scientific Q Exactive, USA) was used to detect the TC degradation intermediates during the catalytic process.

In order to study the biotoxicity of the synthesized SCN/N-CQDs composite photocatalysts leaching solution and TC contaminated aqueous after treatment, a typical gram-negative bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) was selected as the indicator microorganism. The effect of SCN/N-CQDs on the growth of E. coli was investigated by measuring optical density (OD600). Firstly, 5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg SCN/N-CQDs were mixed with 100 mL ultra-pure water. Subsequently, the solution was subjected to dark reaction and light irradiation for 1 h and 1.5 h, respectively. The leaching solution is obtained and stored away from light for later use. Then, E. coli was cultured to logarithmic stage and inoculated in leaching solution of SCN/N-CQDs with different concentrations. 6 mL of SCN/N-CQDs leached solution, 6 mL of bacterial suspension and 38 mL of fresh LB broth were mixed to obtain bacterial suspension, and cultured in 37℃ constant temperature incubator for 15 h. OD value of 600 nm was recorded at 1 h interval to monitor the growth curve of E. coli. The bacterial suspension without SCN/N-CQDs was used as the control. The effect of degradation intermediates on bacterial growth was evaluated by colony counting method. E. coli solution was diluted 106 times after culture to logarithmic stage, 50 μL was evenly spread on the plates with degradation wastewater at different time points (0, 30, 60, 90 min) and original TC, and placed in 37℃ constant temperature incubator for 14 h for viable count. The plates without degradation wastewater were used as the control group. All the experiments were done in triplicate.

The crystallinity and phase structure of SCN/N-CQDs nanocomposites were evaluated by X-ray diffractometer (XRD). As can be seen in Fig. 1 a-b, pure SCN has two distinct diffraction peaks at 2
θ
= 27.74° and 13.30°, respectively, and the peak at 2
θ
= 27.74° (002) is the interlayer stacking of the conjugated aromatic system, while the peak at 2
θ
= 13.30° (001) is an in-plane structural packing associated with tris-triazine [37]. Similar characteristic peaks were found in the XRD patterns of SCN/N-CQDs synthesized with different N-CQDs additions, which indicated that the doped material had a similar structure to the undoped material, and no other impurities were detected. After the addition of N-CQDs, the diffraction peak of (002) shifts to a lower angular value, indicating the interaction between N-CQDs and SCN [36]. In addition, there is no significant N-CQDs diffraction peak in the XRD pattern of the composites, it may be that the addition of N-CQDs caused by less [38,39].

Fig. 1c shows the FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs, SCN and SCN/N-CQDs nanocomposites. It can be seen that the samples of N-CQDs showing an obvious broad absorption band at 3452 cm−1 are compounds with hydroxyl O–H stretching vibrations, which represents the abundant hydroxyl group on the surface of N-CQDs [40]. The characteristic band at 1637 cm−1 is the C = O bending vibration, indicating the presence of the carboxyl group [41]. The FT-IR results further indicated the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on its surface. In the FT-IR spectra of SCN/N-CQDs nanocomposites, the vibrational band at 1000–1700 cm−1 is formed by the C-N stretching vibration in the aromatic ring, and the broad band at 3000–3500 cm−1 corresponds to the free amino group (N–H) and surface adsorption of hydroxyl groups (C-OH) [16,25,42]. The peak at 813 cm−1 is the vibrational mode of the triazine ring of SCN, and the peak at 1409 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching and bending vibrations of C–H [31,43]. The peaks at 3434 cm−1 and 1634 cm−1 are caused by the stretching vibration of the –OH group and the bending vibration of water molecules absorbed on SCN/N-CQDs [44]. The SCN/N-CQDs nanocomposite and SCN exhibited similar spectra, indicating that the addition of N-CQDs has no effect on the chemical structure of SCN.

The composition and chemical states of SCN and SCN/N-CQDs surface elements were further studied by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the XPS spectra showed that C, N, O, and S elements existed in SCN/N-CQDs-1, which were similar to the characteristic peaks of SCN. But the doping amount of S is low, accounting for only 0.47%. The characteristic peak of S is not obvious, and the excess oxygen peak may come from the environment, which is consistent with the results of previous studies [37]. In addition, the C content of SCN/N-CQDs was 48.48%, slightly higher than that of SCN (45.68%), indicating that N-CQDs was successfully introduced. In the high-resolution C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2b), a C–C/C = C peak appears at 284.82 eV, and the peak centered at 286.59 eV corresponds to the C-O bond [20]. After the incorporation of N-CQDs, the derived peak of C-O has a slight displacement (286.38 eV to 286.59 eV), indicating that N-CQDs may be anchored to SCN by C-O bond. The N-C = N peak at 288.29 eV is attributed to sp2 hybrid carbon atoms binding to an n-containing aromatic ring [42]. In the N 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2c), there are four distinct characteristic peaks at 398.79, 400.17, 401.24 and 404.30 eV, which correspond to the C-N = C bond between the sp2 hybridized nitrogen atom and the carbon atom, derived from free amino group (C-NH2) of pentadiazine ring, tertiary nitrogen atom N-(C)3 bonded to three carbon atoms in aromatic ring, and peaks caused by π-π transition, respectively [45-47]. The S 2p spectrum (Fig. 2d) shows four characteristic peaks. A wide peak corresponding to the C-S bond at 164.11 eV, which is formed by the substitution of S atoms by lattice C atoms [48]. The peak at 167.32 eV corresponds to the oxidation S(SOx), and the peak at 171.42 eV is due to the action of the C-SOx part [49-51]. Therefore, the experimental results show that N-CQDs is successfully introduced into the surface of SCN.

The morphological features of SCN and SCN/N-CQDs were examined by field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM). It can be clearly observed from the Fig. 3 , SCN exhibits a uniform hexagonal nanotube with an average side length of about 7.5 μm and a tube length of about 45 μm (Fig. 3a-c). The tubular structure is beneficial to enhance light scattering and active sites, and is also a good carrier for nanomaterials with specific sizes and chemical functional groups [52,53]. SCN/N-CQDs-1 have similar morphological characteristics (Fig. 3d-f). And the middle part of the nanotube presents an incomplete tubular structure generated by the quenching reaction during the growth process [54]. The nanotube structure is generated because melamine is converted into cyanuric acid during the hydrothermal process added before the calcination process, and then an ordered supramolecular aggregated structure is formed through the arrangement of intermolecular H2 bonds, forming a rod-like crystal structure [52]. Energy dispersion spectroscopy (EDS) results show that the signals of C, N, S and other components can be clearly distinguished in SCN/N-CQDs-1 samples, which is consistent with XRD and XPS results, which further indicates that N-CQDs has been successfully introduced into SCN (Fig. 4 a-d). The signal distributions of C and N elements are much wider than those of S elements, indicating that C and N form the basic backbone of the composite.

The morphologies of N-CQDs, SCN, SCN/N-CQDs before and after loading were observed by transmission electron microscope (TEM). The results show that N-CQDs is about 2 nm, and they consisted of monodisperse, spherical, well-separated carbon dots (Fig. 5 a). Fig. 5b-c show that the TEM images of SCN show a regular nanotube structure consistent with the SEM image results, with abundant pores and smooth surface. In Fig. 5d, it can be found that N-CQDs are unevenly distributed on the surface of SCN, and an obvious dense dark spot area can be seen, and no free N-CQDs are found. And in the TEM images of SCN/N-CQDs-1 (Fig. 5e), the N-CQDs showed a tendency to agglomerate, and the closely contacting particles may cause lattice overlap. HRTEM images of SCN/N-CQDs-1 show a planar lattice spacing of 0.204 nm (Fig. 5f), which is consistent with the (101) lattice plane of graphitic carbon and the same as that of N-CQDs [31]. Some studies have shown that carbon nitride is amorphous diffraction and does not have lattice fringes [37]. Therefore, it can be seen that N-CQDs were successfully loaded on SCN.

The surface area and pore size distribution of SCN and SCN/N-CQDs photocatalysts were analyzed by nitrogen adsorption and desorption method. As shown in Fig. 6 , nitrogen adsorption–desorption curve and pore size distribution (illustration) presents type IV isotherms of type H3 hysteresis loop, indicating that it has a mesoporous structure [55]. Compared with SCN, the modification of N-CQDs significantly improved the adsorption capacity of nitrogen. The calculation results of Brunauere-Emmette-Teller (BET) equation show that the specific surface area (22.444 m2/g) and pore volume (0.133 cm3/g) of SCN/N-CQDs slightly greater than the specific surface area (18.688 m2/g) and pore volume (0.128 cm3/g) of SCN. The enhancement of pore volume and specific surface area facilitates the exposure of more active sites and enhances the adsorption and diffusion of reactants and products, thus improving the photocatalytic performance [56].

Due to light absorption characteristics of semiconductor photocatalytic activity one of the important factors is to determine, so by ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectrum of g-C3N4 (CN), SCN and SCN/N-CQDs samples had been used to characterize the optical properties. As shown in Fig. 7 b, the carbonized reaction solution appears brown under visible light and emits blue light under UV light, indicating the generation of N-CQDs. Fig. 7a show that SCN, SCN/N-CQDs-1 have light absorption capacity in the spectrum range from ultraviolet to visible light. However, CN has a weak light absorption capacity in the range of visible light. Importantly, SCN/N-CQDs-1 exhibits a slight redshift and a strong visible-light response. Redshift indicates that the introduction of N-CQDs captures more light energy, thus inducing the generation of more electron-hole pairs [57]. In addition, after doping with sulfur atoms, the band gap of CN is directly reduced, and the light absorption band edge (Eg) is reduced to 2.66 eV, as shown in Fig. 7c. The valence band (VB) value can be obtained from the VB-XPS spectrum [58]. As can be seen from the Fig. 7d, the VB potential of CN and SCN is estimated to be 1.78 eV and 1.34 eV, respectively. Therefore, the conduction band (CB) values of CN and SCN were calculated as −0.99 eV and −1.32 eV, respectively, according to formula
E

E
VB
-
E g
. Where, ECB, EVB and Eg are VB potential, band gap energy and CB potential, respectively.

The photoluminescence (PL), photocurrent transient response (I-t) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) of SCN/N-CQDs were analyzed in order to study the charge transfer and separation behavior of the photogenerated carrier. As shown in the Fig. 7e, the SCN/N-CQDs-1 composite has lower PL strength than CN and SCN at 420 nm excitation light. The results show that the modification of N-CQDs can effectively inhibit the recombination of photoexcited electron-hole pairs [16]. To understand the dynamics of carrier separation, the photoinduced carrier lifetimes of CN, SCN and SCN/N-CQDs-1 were further investigated by time-resolved transient PL spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 7f. By formula

determine synthetic materials fluorescence emission attenuation life (τ).

It can be seen from Table.1 that the average lifetime (τ) of CN and SCN are 13.7693 ns and 7.4049 ns, respectively. And the average lifetime of SCN/N-CQDs-1 decreases significantly to 7.2638 ns. The shortening of the mean lifetime of photogenerated electrons indicates that the modification of N-CQDs can promote transfer and separation of photogenerated carriers and inhibit the recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs.

As shown in the photocurrent transient response curve (I-t) (Fig. 7g), the photocurrent transient response of SCN/N-CQDs-1 is significantly stronger than that of CN and SCN. This is because N-CQDs, as electron acceptors, can effectively capture photoinduced electrons generated by SCN excitation, thus promoting the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) shows that the SCN/N-CQDs-1 nanocomposite has the smallest arc radius (Fig. 7h). The smaller the radius, the lower the charge transfer impedance and the stronger the effective transport capacity of the photogenerated carrier [36]. Moreover, the EIS Nyquist diagram with analog equivalent circuit is shown in Fig. 7h, where R1 is the overall electrolyte resistance and R2 is the charge transfer resistance in parallel with the constant phase Angle element (CPE1). According to the fitting of Nyquist diagram and equivalent circuit diagram, as shown in Table.2 , the charge transfer resistance (R2) of SCN/N-CQDs-1 is estimated to be 1303 Ω, much lower than that of CN (29513 Ω) and SCN (6574 Ω). In conclusion, SCN/N-CQDs-1 has superior photogenerated electron hole pair separation efficiency and more efficient photocatalytic activity.

Under the irradiation of visible light with λ > 400 nm, the photocatalytic activity of SCN/N-CQDs samples against persistent organic pollutants was compared. First, under 1 h dark reaction condition, the adsorption and desorption between TC and the sample reached a balance. As shown in the Fig. 8 a, in the absence of photocatalyst, almost no degradation of TC occurs after 90 min of light irradiation, which means that self-photolysis can be ignored, consistent with previous research results [59]. Under the same conditions, the degradation efficiency of TC by nano-composite materials with different SCN/N-CQDs-0.1, SCN/N-CQDs-1, SCN/N-CQDs-3, SCN/N-CQDs-5 and SCN/N-CQDs-7 was 76.56% and 79.11%, 76.53%, 75.46%, 72.17%, respectively. And the degradation efficiency of composites was significantly higher than that of SCN monomer (58.33%). Among them, SCN/N-CQDs-1 composite has the highest photocatalytic efficiency for TC. In addition, the removal rate of total organic carbon (TOC) by SCN/N-CQDs-1 was 16.98%. The results showed that most tetracycline molecules had been photocatalyzed to other small molecules after the end of photocatalytic degradation. However, a small amount of intermediates are still not fully mineralized in the solution. The modification of N-CQDs effectively expanded the visible light absorption range and promoted the photoinduced carrier transfer, thus enhancing the photocatalytic performance of SCN [60]. However, with the increase of N-CQDs content, the photocatalytic performance of SCN/N-CQDs composites decreases, which is due to excess N-CQDs acting as recombination sites for electron-hole pairs [36].

By analyzing the photodegradation kinetics of TC, the dynamic degradation process of TC was explored. The photocatalytic degradation rate constant can be calculated by first-order kinetic equation:
- l n

C t

)
=
K 1
t
, where C0 and Ct (mg/L) are TC concentration at initial time and time t (min) respectively, K (min−1) is the apparent reaction rate constant, and t (min) is the irradiation time [60]. The degradation rate constant K1 of TC can be obtained from the curve fitting slope value of
- l n (
C t
/
C 0
)
and t, as shown in Fig. 8b. From the slope value of curve fitting line, the rate constants K1 of SCN、SCN/N-CQDs-0.1、SCN/N-CQDs-1、SCN/N-CQDs-3、SCN/N-CQDs-5 and SCN/N-CQDs-7 are 0.0093, 0.0159, 0.017, 0.0165, 0.0155 and 0.0139 min−1, respectively. It can be observed that, compared with SCN, the dynamic constants of in the TC degradation process of the composites with different N-CQDs addition levels are significantly increased. SCN/N-CQDs-1 has the best photocatalytic performance for TC degradation, and the corresponding degradation rate constant (k1 = 0.017 min−1) is about 1.8 times that of SCN (k1 = 0.0093 min−1). The results showed that the incorporation of N-CQDs significantly increased the photocatalytic performance of SCN nanotubes.

In the actual environment, the concentration of antibiotic wastewater will fluctuate in a small range, so the degradation efficiency of SCN/N-CQDs composite material (20 mg) at the initial concentration of 10–30 mg/L TC was studied. As shown in the Fig. 9 a, under the conditions of 10, 15, 20 and 30 mg/L, the degradation rate was negatively correlated with the initial TC concentration, which were 79.11%, 77.77%, 76.28% and 77.70%, respectively. This may be because the TC absorbed by the surface of SCN/N-CQDs nanocomposites increases with the increase of the concentration. Excessive TC covers the active sites on the catalyst surface, which not only reduces the generation of hydroxyl radicals on the catalyst surface, but also prevents the composite material from receiving light to activate the photocatalytic process [60]. The results showed that the degradation rate decreased with the increase of TC concentration, but the photocatalytic performance of TC degradation was not significantly affected.

The amount of catalyst is an important factor affecting the photodegradation rate. Therefore, under the fixed initial concentration of pollutants, the influence of different dosages (0–40 mg) on the photocatalytic activity of composite materials (TC concentration is 10 mg/L) was studied. As shown in the Fig. 9b, with the increase of the amount of photocatalyst, the efficiency of TC degradation increases correspondingly. In the absence of SCN/N-CQDs (i.e., 0 mg), the degradation rate of TC was only 5.9% after 90 min of visible light irradiation. The degradation rate of TC increased to 72.51%, 79.11%, 82.48% and 82.44% after adding 10、20、30 and 40 mg photocatalyst, respectively. However, higher catalyst dosage did not significantly improve TC degradation efficiency. In addition, the TC degradation rate did not change significantly with the change of catalyst dosage in the range of 20–40 mg. This is because as the concentration of photocatalyst increases, the turbidity of the reaction solution increases and the transmittance of visible light decreases. Therefore, less visible light can induce light to produce charge carrying, resulting in the photocatalytic performance of SCN/N-CQDs cannot be further enhanced [61]. From an economic cost point of view, the amount of catalyst used is a key factor in pollutant treatment. Therefore, according to the above results, it can be concluded that the optimal dosage of SCN/N-CQDs catalyst is 20 mg.

In general, pH affects the reactivity of pollutants, the structure of photocatalysts, and the generation of free radicals [47]. Therefore, the effect of SCN/N-CQDs nanocomposites (0.2 g/L) on the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC (20 mg/L) at different pH values (pH = 3.0, 5.0, 7.0, 9.0) was studied. As shown in Fig. 9c, when pH = 5.0, 77.43% TC was removed in 90 min. The degradation rate was 75.81% at pH = 7.0. At pH = 3.0 and 9.0, only 72.03% and 74.29% TC were degraded, respectively. It can be found that under alkaline conditions, the photocatalytic degradation speed is significantly increased. In general, SCN/N-CQDs nanocomposites have better photocatalytic degradation activity under alkaline conditions compared with acidic conditions. Some studies have shown that the abundant hydroxyl group on the surface of the material is one of the reasons for promoting the photocatalytic activity. At lower pH values, the formation of surface hydroxyl protonation leads to the destruction of surface oxygen-containing functional groups [62,63]. The effect of pH on the photocatalytic reaction also depends on the formation of free radicals. At a lower pH value, compared with TC molecules, the photogenerated O2 − free radical is more likely to combine with H+ in the solution to form hydrogen peroxide, and O2 − is consumed in large quantities, thus weakening the degradation efficiency of TC [36]. The alkaline environment is favorable for the reaction of OH− with h+ to generate •OH radical, thus increasing the photocatalytic rate [64]. According to the results, SCN/N-CQDs tetracycline wastewater treatment has a wide range of application (pH 3.0–9.0), which can realize all kinds of high efficient degradation of tetracycline in water environment.

In practical wastewater, there are usually many inorganic anions, which may affect the efficiency of photocatalytic degradation. Anions such as Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and PO4 3− were added to TC solution to further study the effect of co-existing inorganic anions on TC degradation efficiency. As shown in the Fig. 9d, Cl−, SO4 2−, NO3 − and PO4 3− anions all showed different degrees of inhibition. Combined with the research results on the effect of TC initial pH value on the catalytic performance of SCN/N-CQDs, the addition of SO4 2−, NO3 − and PO4 3− anions will change the pH value of TC solution, thus affecting the degradation efficiency of TC to a certain extent. Among them, SO4 2− ions react with photoluminescent holes (h+) to form sulfate radicals. Since S is a strong oxidant, sulfate free radical can accelerate photocatalytic degradation [65]. Therefore, the effect of SO4 2− on the photocatalytic activity of SCN/N-CQDs was minimal, and the degradation rate was only reduced by 3.3%. In the presence of NO3 −, the degradation rate of TC decreased to 75.51%. Because of NO3 − will capture OH free radicals in the photocatalytic reaction system, leading to the annihilation of free radicals and light catalytic efficiency lower. When there is NO3 − and SO4 2−, the degradation rates of TC decreased to 75.81% and 75.51%, respectively. The inhibitory effect of NO3 − is due to its ability to act as a scavenger for OH radicals, but their adsorption capacity on the surface of the material is weak, so the NO3 − on photocatalytic degradation efficiency of TC only has a minor influence [36]. In the process of photocatalysis, Cl− and TC have adsorption competition on the surface of SCN/N-CQDs, and Cl− can be oxidized by OH radical to chlorinated organic compounds with lower activity [66]. Therefore, the presence of Cl− has a greater inhibitory effect on the degradation of TC, which is reduced from 79.11% to 72.68%. The PO4 3− tetrahedral structure makes it more competitive for the functional groups on the surface of SCN/N-CQDs, which has a significant impact on the degradation performance of TC [67].

Dissolved organic matter (DOM), as a common substance in wastewater, may interfere with the photocatalytic performance of composites. Therefore, taking humic acid (HA) as the representative of organic macromolecules, the effect of HA at different concentrations on the photocatalytic degradation performance of SCN/N-CQDs was studied. The results were shown in Figure.S1, after 90 min, TC degradation rates were 79.11%, 68.06%, 47.25% and 39.58% when HA content was 0 mg/L, 10 mg/L, 20 mg/L and 30 mg/L, respectively. With the increase of HA concentration, TC degradation rate decreased gradually. On the one hand, this is because HA will have adsorption competitiveness with TC. When the concentration of HA increases, its adsorption competitiveness on the surface of SCN/N-CQDs becomes stronger, which leads to the decrease of the photocatalytic degradation rate of TC by SCN/N-CQDs [68]. On the other hand, HA will compete with the photocatalyst for light absorption. The higher the HA concentration, the more effective photon number absorbed, thus reducing the utilization of effective photon energy by SCN/N-CQDs, leading to the degradation performance weakened [69].

In order to explore the internal reaction mechanism of different active substances in photocatalytic degradation process, the free radical capture experiment of TC degradation by SCN/N-CQDs was carried out. About 79.11% of TC was degraded without the addition of scavengers. L-Ascorbic Acid, EDTA-2Na and isopropanol were selected as the quenching agents of superoxide radical (O2 −), hole (h+) and hydroxyl radical (OH), respectively. The Fig. 10 a shows the photocatalytic performance of TC degradation in the presence of different quenching agents. When L-Ascorbic Acid was added, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of SCN/N-CQDs on TC under visible light significantly decreased by 13.00%, indicating that O2 − plays a leading role in the decomposition reaction of TC. In the presence of EDTA-2Na, the degradation rate of TC decreased to 54.69%, decreasing by 24.42%, indicating that photogenerated h+ is the main factor affecting the photocatalytic degradation of SCN/N-CQDs. In addition, the addition of isopropyl alcohol also showed an inhibitory effect on the degradation of TC, and the degradation efficiency decreased by 5.65%, indicating that OH radical was one of the species involved in the photocatalytic degradation of TC by SCN/N-CQDs. Therefore, the results showed that the three active substances were involved in the TC degradation reaction, and their importance was in the order of O2 −> h+> OH.

In order to further explore the generation of active species in SCN/N-CQDs system under visible light irradiation, we conducted electron spin resonance (ESR) test on the active species in the reaction process. 5, 5–dimethyl–1–pyrroline N–oxide (DMPO) reagent is usually used to detect O2 − in methanol and OH in ultrapure water, 2,2,6, 6-tetramethylpiperidine nitrogen-oxide (TEMPO) reagent was used to detect h+ [70]. 2,2,6,6-etrarnethyl-4-piperldone-1-oxyl (TEMPONE) was used to capture 1O2 [71,72]. As shown in the Fig. 10b, there were no significant signal peaks in the dark environment, but in the methanol dispersion process under visible light, four characteristic peaks belonging to DMPO– O2 − active substances were shown. Moreover, DMPO– O2 − peak intensity increased significantly with the extension of illumination time, indicating that O2 − was formed in the process of TC degradation by SCN/N-CQDs under visible light irradiation. In addition, from the Fig. 10c can observe DMPO– OH four strong characteristic signal of active material, and with the extension of light irradiation time, the corresponding peak intensity increases. The results showed that OH was produced in the SCN/N-CQDs photocatalytic reaction system for the degradation of pollutants. In particular, TEMPO- h+ showed a strong signal of active substance under dark conditions. However, the signal of TEMPO- h+ active substance became weaker as the duration of illumination increased Fig. 10d. This is because the free radical trapping agent added will react with h+. With the increase of light time, the hole increases, the capture agent consumed by h+ increases, the capture agent signal becomes weaker, and the peak becomes weaker. In addition, as shown in Fig. S2a, the signal intensity of TEMPO- h+ active substance under nitrogen gas condition was almost the same as that without N2 exhaust. It can be inferred that TEMPO signals in SCN/CQDs systems are not formed by the combination of TEMP and dissolved oxygen. Therefore, the results show that h+ is produced in the photocatalytic reaction of SCN/N-CQDs. Furthermore, in Fig. S2b, the TEMPONE-1O2 signal of active material can not be observed, which means that the 1O2 is not produced for TC degradation reaction. These results indicate that in the photocatalytic degradation system of SCN/N-CQDs, the efficient degradation of TC is achieved through the free radical mechanism of the interaction of O2 −, OH and h+, which is consistent with the results of free radical capture experiment.

The band gap (Eg), valence band (EVB) and conduction band (ECB) values of SCN are 2.66 eV, 1.34 eV and − 1.32 eV respectively according to UV–Vis DRS, VB-XPS spectra and formulas. Therefore, we can propose a possible mechanism to improve the TC removal efficiency of SCN/N-CQDs composite photocatalyst, as shown in Fig. 11 . SCN with hexagonal tubular structure have strong light scattering ability and provide a large number of active sites. In addition, the coupling between N-CQDs and SCN nanotubes greatly increases the spectrum absorption range. Under visible light, photoinduced electrons jump from the SCN valence band (VB) to the conduction band (CB), leaving corresponding holes (h+) in the VB. Because the N atom in N-CQDs has a strong electron affinity, the photogenerated electrons in the SCN conduction band are easily transferred to N-CQDs [73]. Therefore, N-CQDs can be used as an electron acceptor to accelerate the separation and transfer of photogenerated carriers. The conduction band energy levels of N-CQDs and SCNs are more negative than the reduction potential of oxygen (E(O2/ O2 −) = − 0.046 eV), so electrons on the conduction bands of N-CQDs and SCNs can reduce O2 to a large amount of O2 −. The valence band value of SCN (1.34 eV) is more negative than the standard redox potential of E(OH/OH−) = 2.40 eV, and the residual holes cannot directly oxidize OH– or H2O to generate OH, but can directly degrade TC molecules adsorbed on the catalyst surface [36]. However, ESR and capture experiments show that OH radical exists in the reaction process, which indicates that O2 − has been further reduced and combined with H2O molecules to generate OH radical. TC molecules adsorbed on the surface of SCN/N-CQDs composites are induced by light to generate OH radicals and O2 − radicals, which are decomposed into carbon dioxide and water.

High performance liquid chromatography mass spectrometer (HPLC − MS) was used to investigate the intermediate products in the TC degradation process, to infer the TC degradation pathway. As shown in Figure.S3, under the condition of light, the TC of the characteristic peak (m/z = 445) slowly disappear, and produced some intermediate decomposition. The possible degradation pathway of TC is shown in Fig. 12 . TC molecules first generate P1 (m/z = 467) through ring-opening reaction, and then generate P2 intermediates with m/z = 437 through demethylation, oxidation and dehydrogenation reaction [74]. Compound P3 (m/z = 104) was formed by further oxidation of the six-membered ring structure. In addition, due to the separation of methyl, amino and ketone, TC is degraded into intermediates of P4 (m/z = 415), P5 (m/z = 397) and P6 (m/z = 360). The cyclic hydrocarbon structure of compound P6 (m/z = 360) is destroyed to form P7 (m/z = 318) and P15 (m/z = 338) [75]. Then P7 (m/z = 318) loses its methyl group to form compound P8 (m/z = 274), which forms intermediates P9 (m/z = 228), P10 (m/z = 149), and P11 (m/z = 127) through further oxidation decomposition and ring-opening reaction. In addition, P7 (m/z = 318) undergoes a process of dehydration and decarbonization to form compound P12 (m/z = 301). P13 (m/z = 282) and P14 (m/z = 249) were formed by ring-opening and dehydrogenation of P12 (m/z = 301) [76,77]. At the same time, the destruction of cyclic hydrocarbon structure and loss of methyl group in compound P6 (m/z = 360) promoted the formation of P16 (m/z = 344) and P17 (m/z = 334). Finally, the intermediates produced during the photocatalytic degradation of TC are decomposed into harmless molecules such as CO2 and H2O under the strong oxidation action of free radicals.

In practical applications, the chemical stability and cyclability of catalysts are important influencing factors. Therefore, the stability and reusability of SCN/N-CQDs were studied by cyclic experiments. Results As shown in Fig. 13 a, after five consecutive cycles, the degradation efficiency (C/C0) of TC was 78.47%, 77.63%, 78.21%, 77.42% and 76.67%, respectively. SCN/N-CQDs showed satisfactory reusable performance. The XRD pattern of the catalyst after 5 cycles of TC degradation showed very similar diffraction peaks to the original catalyst (Fig. 13b). This indicated that the structure of SCN/N-CQDs did not change significantly after photocatalytic degradation. The chemical composition changes of the recycled SCN/N-CQDs samples were determined by XPS analysis. As can be seen in Fig. 13c that the recycled SCN/N-CQDs contain S 2p, O 1 s, C 1 s and N 1 s peaks. Among them, the proportion of oxygen atoms on the surface of SCN/N-CQDs increased from 1.54% to 2.38% after cycling, indicating that slight oxidation reaction occurred in the SCN/N-CQDs system, but no qualitative change. Moreover, it can be observed from Fig. 13d-f that the C, N and S elements in SCN/N-CQDs after photocatalytic reaction are consistent with the initial XPS fine spectrum results, which indicates that the chemical composition of SCN/N-CQDs photocatalyst does not change in quality after TC degradation. The results show that the SCN/N-CQDs composite catalyst has good chemical stability and reusability. Therefore, SCN/N-CQDs are a promising material for practical applications.

It can be seen from Fig. 14 a that E. coli is in the mature stage after culturing for 13 h. In the first 6 h, after adding different concentrations of SCN/N-CQDs leaching solution, the OD600 value was almost the same as that of the original E. coli. Within 6–12 h of culture, the OD600 value of E. coli bacteria liquid with leaching solution was higher than the original E. coli. The results showed that the leaching solution of SCN/N-CQDs composite materials did not inhibit the growth of E. coli, and even slightly promoted the growth from the late logarithmic phase to the mature stage. Therefore, it can be concluded that the SCN/N-CQDs composite is stable, and its leaching solution does not cause biological toxicity to E. coli.

The effect of the products of SCN/N-CQDs degrading TC on the growth of E. coli was further studied by colony counting method. As shown in the photo of E. coli colony in Fig. 14b, no E. coli colony appeared in the plate supplemented with original TC and the degradation products at 0 min, because TC, one of the antibiotics, had certain inactivation and growth inhibition effects on bacteria [78]. In the plates with TC degradation products at 30, 60, and 90 min time points, E. coli colonies grew well, indicating that TC had low activity after photodegradation for half an hour or more, and had no side effects.

The inhibition rate of birth growth was calculated by the number of E. coli colonies to investigate the effect of TC degradation products by SCN/N-CQDs on bacterial growth. As shown in Fig. 14c, the inhibition rates of E. coli bacteria were 9.296%, 5.052% and 8.685%, respectively, in the plate with 30–90 min photodegradation wastewater added. The inhibition rate was much lower than that of the plate with original TC and without photodegradation solution. This indicates that TC can be effectively decomposed by SCN/N-CQDs photocatalytic degradation, but the residual TC still has a certain inhibitory effect on the growth of E. coli. It was further verified that TC photocatalytic degradation products had no biotoxicity to E. coli by the number of E. coli colonies and inhibition rate.

Through the number of E. coli colonies and the inhibition rate, it can be further verified that the wastewater after the products of photocatalytic degradation of TC by SCN/N-CQDs is not biotoxic to E. coli.

In this paper, the SCN/N-CQDs composite photocatalyst with high catalytic activity was synthesized by coupling pig manure derived N-CQDs on the surface of SCN hexagonal nanotubes by solvothermal method. Compared with pure SCN, complex SCN/N-CQDs showed better photocatalytic degradation of TC. After 90 min of photocatalytic reaction, the degradation efficiency of SCN/N-CQDs for TC was 20.78% higher than that of SCN monomer. This is because the addition of N-CQDs expands the spectrum utilization range, promotes the formation of photogenerated electron hole pairs, increases the specific surface area of the material, and thus enhances the photocatalytic activity. Through the exploration of catalyst addition amount, initial pollutant concentration, pH value, coexisting ions, humus and other reaction parameters, it is proved that SCN/N-CQDs has broad application prospect in organic wastewater treatment. In addition, the results of free radical capture and ESR experiments show that h+, OH and O2 −are the main active substances in the photocatalytic degradation process, with the influence degree of O2 −>h+> OH. In addition, SCN/N-CQDs has good chemical stability and reusability. After five cycles, it can still degrade 76.67% of tetracycline, and its structure and chemical composition have no significant changes. Finally, the biotoxicity results showed that the leachate and degradation products of SCN/N-CQDs composite material did not produce biotoxicity to the growth of E. coli. The inhibition rate of the products after photodegradation for 90 min was only 8.685%, which was much lower than that of pure tetracycline and undegraded solution. In conclusion, SCN/N-CQDs photocatalyst is a kind of water pollution treatment material with practical application prospect.
